request.question: Who is the director of Scared to Death, Irvin Kershner or William Malone?

title: Supernova (2000 film)
Never Say Never Again
The Empire Strikes Back
The Luck of Ginger Coffey (novel)
RoboCop 2
Scared to Death (1981 film)
Irvin Kershner
Stakeout on Dope Street
Paul Coates
William Malone (director)
sentences: Supernova is a 2000 Swiss-American science fiction horror film written by David C. Wilson, William Malone and Daniel Chuba and directed by Walter Hill, credited as "Thomas Lee."
"Thomas Lee" was chosen as a directorial pseudonym for release, as the name Alan Smithee had become too well known as a badge of a film being disowned by its makers.
It was originally developed in 1988 by director William Malone as "Dead Star," with paintings by H. R. Giger and a plot that had been called ""Hellraiser" in outer space."
Jack Sholder was hired for substantial uncredited reshoots, and Francis Ford Coppola was brought in for editing purposes.
Various sources suggest that little of Hill's work remains in the theatrical cut of the film.
The film shares several plot similarities with the film "Event Horizon," released in 1997, and "Alien Cargo," released in 1999.
The cast featured James Spader, Angela Bassett, Robert Forster, Lou Diamond Phillips, Peter Facinelli, Robin Tunney, and Wilson Cruz.
This film was shot by cinematographer Lloyd Ahern and scored by composers David C. Williams and Burkhard Dallwitz.

Never Say Never Again is a 1983 James Bond spy film starring Sean Connery, directed by Irvin Kershner, produced by Jack Schwartzman, and written by Lorenzo Semple Jr. with uncredited additional co-writers Dick Clement and Ian La Frenais, from a story by Kevin McClory, Jack Whittingham, and Ian Fleming.
It is the second adaptation of Fleming's "Thunderball", which was previously adapted as the 1965 film of the same name.

Unlike the majority of James Bond films, "Never Say Never Again" was not produced by Eon Productions, but by an independent production company, one of whose members was Kevin McClory, one of the original writers of the "Thunderball" story, who retained the filming rights of the novel following a legal dispute over them.
The Empire Strikes Back (also known as Star Wars: Episode V – The Empire Strikes Back) is a 1980 American epic space opera film directed by Irvin Kershner.
Leigh Brackett and Lawrence Kasdan wrote the screenplay, with George Lucas writing the film's story and serving as executive producer.

The second installment in the original "Star Wars" trilogy, it was produced by Gary Kurtz for Lucasfilm and stars Mark Hamill, Harrison Ford, Carrie Fisher, Billy Dee Williams, Anthony Daniels, David Prowse, Kenny Baker, Peter Mayhew, and Frank Oz.
The Luck of Ginger Coffey, a novel by Northern Irish-Canadian writer Brian Moore, was published in 1960, in the United States by "The Atlantic Monthly" and in the United Kingdom by Andre Deutsch.
In Canada, it received a Governor General's Award.
The book was made into a film, directed by Irvin Kershner, and released in 1964.
Robert Shaw starred in the title role.
RoboCop 2 is a 1990 American cyberpunk action film directed by Irvin Kershner and starring Peter Weller, Nancy Allen, Dan O'Herlihy, Belinda Bauer, Tom Noonan and Gabriel Damon.
Set in the near future in a dystopian metropolitan Detroit, Michigan, it is the sequel to the 1987 film, "RoboCop".

Scared to Death, also known as The Aberdeen Experiment and Scared to Death: Syngenor (DVD sleeve re-title), is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film directed by William Malone and starring John Stinson.
Irvin Kershner (born Isadore Kershner; April 29, 1923November 27, 2010) was an American actor and director of film and television.

He gained notice early in his career as a filmmaker for directing quirky, independent films early in his career, later moving on to films such "The Empire Strikes Back", the James Bond adaptation "Never Say Never Again", and "RoboCop 2".
Stakeout on Dope Street is a 1958 crime film directed by Irvin Kershner and written by Andrew J. Fenady, Irvin Kershner and Irwin Schwartz.
It follows three teenagers who inadvertently get themselves involved in a drug ring.
It was the directorial debut of Kershner.
The film stars Yale Wexler, Abby Dalton, Morris Miller, Allen Kramer and Jonathon Haze.
The film was released by Warner Bros. on May 3, 1958.
Paul V. Coates (March 10, 1921 – November 16, 1968) was an American print and television journalist.
He was known for his popular daily newspaper column and as the host of the syndicated tabloid-style television series "Confidential File", developed by Coates and Irvin Kershner (1923–2010).
William Malone (born 1953) is an American horror filmmaker who directed such films as the 1999 remake "House on Haunted Hill", "Scared to Death", "Creature", and "FeardotCom".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.summary_1:
Scared to Death (film) — director attribution: William Malone vs Irvin Kershner

- "William Malone (born 1953)" — passage [1] identifies him as an American horror filmmaker and lists "Scared to Death" among films he "directed".
- Passage [2] states the film "is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film directed by William Malone" and lists alternate titles "The Aberdeen Experiment" and "Scared to Death: Syngenor".
- "Irvin Kershner" — passage [3] shows he "was an American actor and director of film and television" and credits include "The Empire Strikes Back", "Never Say Never Again", and "RoboCop 2"; no passage links Kershner to Scared to Death.
- Ambiguity/missing: passage [2] title shows "Scared to Death (1981 film)" while its text says "1980" — year variant ambiguous; film title has alternate variants. Bridge entity: the film page ("Scared to Death" with year/variant) would resolve year/title variants.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
  1. No additional retrieval needed (passages already attribute the film to William Malone); otherwise fetch "Scared to Death (film)" page (check lede/infobox and "Cast"/"Production") to confirm year/alternate titles.
  2. If verification desired, fetch "William Malone (director)" page lede/filmography.
  3. If needed, fetch "Irvin Kershner" page filmography/infobox to confirm absence of this title.

hop2_docs: Scared to Death (1981 film) | Scared to Death, also known as The Aberdeen Experiment and Scared to Death: Syngenor (DVD sleeve re-title), is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film directed by William Malone and starring John Stinson.

Peter Hyams | Peter Hyams (born July 26, 1943) is an American film director, screenwriter and cinematographer, known for directing "Capricorn One", the 1981 science fiction thriller "Outland", "2010" (the sequel to Stanley Kubrick's ""), the 1986 action/comedy "Running Scared", the comic book adaptation "Timecop", the action film "Sudden Death" (both starring Jean-Claude Van Damme), and the horror films "The Relic" and "End of Days".

You Scare Me to Death | You Scare Me to Death is a posthumous album credited to Marc Bolan of T. Rex.  Released in 1981 by record label Cherry Red, it is first LP of material released after his death in 1977.
Running Scared (1980 film) | Running Scared is a 1980 action/adventure film directed by Paul Glickler, starring Ken Wahl and Judge Reinhold, and introducing Annie McEnroe.

Syngenor | Syngenor is a 1990 B horror/science fiction movie that was a sequel to the 1981 film "Scared to Death".  The film was directed by George Elanjian Jr and written by Michael Carmody and Brent V. Friedman.  It features David Gale and Kathryn Noble.

Scared to Death | Scared to Death is a 1947 thriller film directed by Christy Cabanne and starring Bela Lugosi.  The picture was filmed in Cinecolor.  The film is historically important as the only color film in which Bela Lugosi has a starring role.
Scared to Death (disambiguation) | Scared to Death is a 1947 horror film starring Béla Lugosi.
